claim: One of the things we’ve seen related to regulatory — it massively reduces change aversion and improves iteration.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: Example: let’s say you’re going to certify an airplane.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: One of the zillions of things you have to do is prove it can withstand a lightning strike.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: The regulatory documentation for the test plan stretches on for, say, 200 pages.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: What you would classically do is hire a — let’s be honest — not super-bright engineer who’s willing to be there, monkey at keyboard, writing 200 pages of regulatory compliance documentation.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: And by the way, if you change the airplane, now you want to cry, because there’s another two months of rework of this rote compliance documentation.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: What we’ve found is we can build a RAG that will enable us to basically prompt our way through all of that work in — let’s call it minutes.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: The first-order effect is you save a lot of time.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: The second-order effect is, if you change the specification of the airplane, it now takes minutes, not months.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: And the third-order effect is you can get rid of the not-very-great engineers and have a small number of really creative ones who can iterate rapidly, because the cost of change goes down.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: In a certain sense, the entire regulatory burden — which really hurts the ability to iterate — drops away.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: This is a really undersold story in AI right now.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: The consensus in Silicon Valley is that regulation sucks — we want to go faster, we want to realize this amazing future, we want abundance, prosperity, and stuff that slows down that future is to be avoided.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: It’s totally crazy what goes into building any physical thing in a lot of places.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: But a lot of the regulations themselves are not the problem.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: If you’ve actually read a lot of these things — having non-smog-choked cities is great.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: The problem is that it’s really difficult for humans to deal with understanding and complying with this, and every time you have to exchange a letter with the government, you wait months.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: If you could take a lot of the things we’ve learned and make them totally frictionless, that would be pretty cool.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: Until the regulator starts spewing tokens back at us.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Then you start getting huge amounts of documents from the regulators that you have to comply with, and it’s agent-on-agent wars.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: I’d actually argue that would be an improvement from where we are now.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: One of the terrible things right now is, if you’re going to build anything physical, you have to get a building permit.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: The worst thing we’ve run into is the fire department, because they have the moral imprimatur of people pulling people out of burning buildings — and yet what they actually do is just screw with your design for buildings for months.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: If we could replace the fire marshal with an agent that would critique your building plan quickly — even if its feedback were overdone — it would be massively better than the delays that exist today.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: When Max was talking about this potentially being a good thing — that we have all this regulation — my head went to: the thing that makes agents successful is humans or other agents setting up the right testing guardrails.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: People are really excited about slash goal, or Ralph loops, where you tell the model, “Go do this, and this is your exit criteria.” I’m telling Blake, “Go make us all supersonic.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: Your exit criteria is that you’ve complied with all of these regulations.” There’s totally a world where we say the regulations are great — they’re like our test suite.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: As long as passing them doesn’t incur contradictions, and the regulations are actually reasonable, they’re an awesome guardrail.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: Otherwise we’d be shipping slop directly into the air.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: This is going to turn into a Red Queen’s race.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: They’re going to have agents, we’re going to have agents.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: I think we might have better agents — that’s good, as opposed to human-versus-human.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: But their cycle time, their response time, may get longer.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: I’m sure the patent office is drowning in spam.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: These agencies are going to be slow adopters of AI.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: They’re going to get DDoSed by clever entrepreneurs just overloading them with documents.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: It’s possible the approval time for this stuff may extend out as it suddenly gets flooded.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: It creates an opportunity to really shift the regulatory model.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: Imagine if we drove around a city the way we build things today.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: Before you could go anywhere, you’d have to write a plan, ship it to some regulator, and wait.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: And yet that is absolutely the way we build physical infrastructure in this country.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: We were responsible, we did clinical trials, we reported all the data, but —
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: In fact, the two biggest advancements in tech in Silicon Valley in the last decade — AI and, before that, crypto — they’re both in the math domain, because that’s the last unregulated domain.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: When they start regulating frontier models and start regulating GPUs, that stops as well.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Well, it’s been held back by huge regulatory barriers.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: You can always find a scary case — a vaccine, or a famous medical disaster — but the regulations spread everywhere, the tentacles are everywhere, and there are all these contradictory regulatory bodies.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: SpaceX got sued for not having enough — I forget what — migrants or refugees or whatever, but they’re not allowed to hire them, by government regulation on the other side, because they’re not citizens.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: This is not like logical code that has to compile in one place.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: These are made-up random regulations all over the place.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: You might comply with one state and violate another, violate federal over here, annoy this guy over here, that guy chooses to prosecute one out of fifty people who are his friend.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: I think some of this makes us safer in the sense that the NRC makes us safer — which is that their job was to make sure nuclear energy was safe, and they did this by permitting zero plants from the seventies until I think a year ago.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: I want to be really clear — I’m on the side of deregulation on a lot of this.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: The reality is that this is reflective of the beliefs of the American people.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: There’s a trade-off between the perception of risk taken in human-subjects research, and the rate at which we get new medicines.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: If you approve a bad thing, your career is over.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: I think that is the most important problem to solve in the regulatory state.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: We poll some of the stuff we’re working on in the future to understand where the American people are on it.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: If you push too hard, you can work around it — go to Próspera, all kinds of ways to try to go faster.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: That’s deeper than just saying, “We need regulatory reform.”
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: You’ll see this on X all the time — people are like, “This politician, that politician, the other politician.” They’re elected, by majority vote.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: France — there’s a French entrepreneur on X lamenting that 57% of GDP gets sucked up by the government, so you can’t create companies.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: The Economist just did a little piece — economists are finally coming back around to being capitalists after thirty years — on how the US is outstripping everybody, growing faster, getting bigger.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: But they immediately turn around and say, “It’s because of the oceans, because of natural resources” — everything but capitalism.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: They can’t envision or imagine what could have been if we had just been a little more laissez-faire, a little more open.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Different regulations, different tax structures.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Right now federal tax structure and federal regulations dominate everything.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: But imagine you could go to some small state if you had cancer, and you could try every drug everyone was cooking up.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Same for aircraft — a little harder, because you’ve got to cross a lot of areas — but yeah.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: There’s something magical in there — the notion of innovation zones.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: But if you create opt-in YIMBY zones, they create that experimentation framework.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: Two, you need to prevent the global regulator from casting doubt on what might happen with their clinical trial if they give you some.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: There are specific ways you could really accelerate innovation with a relatively light regulatory touch by just preventing this paranoia from driving our decisions.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: Is there anything better than the FDA out there?
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: What are we benchmarking these regulators against?
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: First, Europe — not really better than the FDA, but they have a different system.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: They’ve got these notified bodies — basically private businesses blessed by their host governments to certify things.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: The notified-body system creates slightly better incentives at the review layer because they can hire people, they can grow, there’s competition.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: They themselves have to be compliant with conditions placed by host governments, but it means there can be many thousands more reviewers than in the US.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: Second — there actually is one approved, getting-paid implantable BCI today, which is in China.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: They have a system that I think is going to give us a run for our money if we’re not careful.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: The costs to bring a drug or device to market are just much lower.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: You can try things in humans and try things on market.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: Here’s the thing I’ve been spending a lot of time thinking about.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: Twenty years ago we were buying far fewer laptops and phones; each one was much more expensive.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: Now they’re cheaper, there are far more of them, we buy more of them, total spending has gone up.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: Stock prices of Qualcomm and Samsung and Apple are way up.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: They’re using the excess wealth generated by phones and laptops to buy more phones and laptops.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: Because of the reimbursement mechanism — there’s this enterprise sale happening — the bucket of money we use to buy healthcare is basically fixed.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: It is not increasing as there’s more stuff producing better healthcare outcomes, the way we see in technological growth industries.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: The rate of spending on healthcare grows at roughly the rate of growth of tax receipts.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: If AI is booming and there are major advances, and two years from now we’re spending ten times as much on AI, this could be great.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: But if in two years we’re spending ten times as much on healthcare, this would be a catastrophe.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: This is fundamentally at odds with being a technological growth industry.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: There’s this omni-problem in healthcare, all related to the same thing: it’s just too expensive to bring these things to market.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: The way out of this is not single-payer or some revision to health insurance.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: It’s to bring down the costs so that someone can buy this with a credit card, finance it, maybe like a car, worst case — and then you charge them in the transaction.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: To do that, we have to make it cheaper to bring these things to market.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: That will allow them to sell these things for $10,000 instead of $100,000.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: Fundamentally, there’s no private market in healthcare.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: The analogy people make sometimes — imagine that instead of going to restaurants and paying, you’d go to all the restaurants, and at the end of the month you’d send all the receipts and bills to your insurer or to the government, and they would reimburse you.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: There’d be a line outside every good restaurant.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: You’re basically running a small communist society inside a larger capitalist society.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: It’s also what we’re doing on roads, which is why we have traffic.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: There’s no variable pricing for getting on the highway, which is why it’s always clogged.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: If you want to step on the third rail of healthcare for a moment, think about this plan.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Imagine that the first 20% of your annual income was your healthcare deductible.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Whatever your annual income is, the first 20% is your healthcare deductible.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: The rest is paid by the government and the insurance system, up to the usual caps they have today.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: You’d create a private market pretty quickly.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: In dental, plastic surgery, a lot of optional medical procedures, you’d get a competitive situation.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Look at dental with veneers and braces and dental surgery.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Those fields do seem to be advancing because they’re private payers — people voting with their money.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: We need to do some equivalent of that in the normal healthcare system.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: They don’t even want to think one step ahead.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: “No, no, no, what about the broke person?” The broke person has no income.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: “Twenty percent is too much for some people.” Okay, you can put some deductible in there.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: But generally, if you don’t have some private market where people are paying out of pocket for what are medical procedures, you’re just not going to get this feedback loop.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: You’re not going to get this ability to spend more money into the system.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Right now, very wealthy people can spend voluntarily into the system.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: If you go shopping for medical care and you want to pay out of pocket, sometimes they’ll quote you a price that’s 10x what they charge the insurance company.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: This is where AI should really shine, and democratize what you can actually do when you find yourself in that situation.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch
